title_en: The Sixth Stage of Grief Is Retro-Computing (2014)

note:
尽管在过去的100年里进行了所有的深海探险，并从海底采集了样本，但人类对海洋最深处的了解仍然很少。而且有很好的理由去了解更多。
大多数海啸都始于海底以下或附近的地震。海底为鱼类、珊瑚以及复杂的微生物、甲壳类和其他生物群落提供了栖息地。它的地形控制着分配热量的气流，有助于调节地球气候。
7月30日是地质学家和海洋学家玛丽·萨普(Marie Tharp)诞辰100周年，她创作的地图改变了人们对世界三分之二地区的想象。从1957年开始，萨普和她的研究伙伴、地质学家布鲁斯·希岑(Bruce Heezen)开始出版第一张全面的地图，显示了海底的主要特征-山脉、山谷和海沟。
作为一名地球科学家，我认为萨普应该和简·古德尔或尼尔·阿姆斯特朗一样出名。原因如下。
直到20世纪50年代，许多科学家都认为海床毫无特色。萨普显示，它包含了崎岖的地形，而且大部分都是以系统的方式进行布局的。
她的照片对板块构造理论的发展至关重要-板块或地壳的大片相互作用，产生了地球的地震和火山活动。早期的研究人员，特别是地球物理学家和气象学家阿尔弗雷德·韦格纳(Alfred Wegener)注意到非洲和南美的海岸线是多么紧密地结合在一起，并提出这两个大陆曾经是相连的。Tharp发现了大西洋中心的山脉和一个裂谷，在那里两个大陆可能被撕裂了。
多亏了萨普手绘的海底再现，我可以想象从纽约步行穿过大西洋海底到里斯本的情景。这次旅行会把我带到大陆架上。然后向下驶向索姆深海平原。我需要绕过水下的山，叫做海山。然后，我会开始缓慢攀登大西洋中部山脉，这是一条南北走向的水下山脉。
在攀登到低于海平面8200英尺的山脊顶峰后，我会下降几百英尺，穿过山脊的中心裂谷，然后沿着山脊的东边往上走。然后又回到了海底，直到我开始沿着欧洲大陆的斜坡长途跋涉到达里斯本。总步行约3800英里-几乎是阿巴拉契亚小径长度的两倍。
萨普出生于密歇根州的伊普西兰蒂，在大学里学习英语和音乐。但后来在1943年，她报名参加了密歇根大学的一个硕士学位项目，该项目旨在培养女性在第二次世界大战期间成为石油地质学家。
萨普后来回忆说：“需要女孩来填补空缺的职位，因为男孩们都出去打架了。”
在为俄克拉何马州的一家石油公司工作后，萨普于1948年在哥伦比亚大学寻找了一份地质学工作。女性不能登上考察船，但萨普可以征兵，并受雇帮助男性研究生。
萨普与研究生希岑(Heezen)合作，希岑给出了她要起草的海底概况。这些是长长的纸卷，用声纳从船上测量，显示沿着线性路径的海底深度。
萨普从一张大白纸开始，标出了纬度和经度的线条。然后，她会仔细地标出船经过的地方。接下来，她将读取声纳剖面上每个位置的深度，将其标记在船的航迹上，并创建自己的压缩剖面，显示到海底的深度与船行驶的距离。
她的一项重要创新是绘制了描绘海底面貌的草图。这些视图使得可视化海底地形和创建地形图变得更容易。
萨普小心翼翼地绘制了横跨北大西洋的六条东西剖面，揭示了以前没有人描述过的东西：海洋中心有一个几英里宽、几百英尺深的裂缝。萨普认为这是一个裂谷-一种已知存在于陆地上的长槽。
希岑称这个想法是“女孩子的谈话”，并告诉萨普重新计算和重新起草。当她这么做的时候，裂谷还在那里。
另一名研究助理正在相同大小和比例的地图上绘制地震震中位置。对比两张地图，希岑和萨普意识到地震震中落在裂谷内。这一发现对板块构造理论的发展至关重要：它表明裂谷正在发生运动，大陆实际上可能正在漂移。
这一见解是革命性的。1957年，刚刚获得博士学位的希岑在普林斯顿大学发表演讲，展示了裂谷和震中，地质系主任哈里·赫斯(Harry Hess)回答说：“你动摇了地质学的基础。”
1959年，美国地质学会出版了希岑、萨普和他们工作的拉蒙特地质天文台主任莫里斯·尤因(Maurice“Doc”Ewing)的“海洋地板：一、北大西洋”(The Floans of the Ocean：I.The North Atlantic)一书。它包含了Tharp的海洋概况、想法和访问Tharp的地形图的权限。
一些科学家认为这项工作很出色，但大多数人不相信。法国海底探险家雅克·库斯托决心证明萨普是错的。乘坐他的科考船“卡利普索号”航行时，他故意越过中大西洋海脊，放下了一台水下电影摄影机。令库斯托惊讶的是，这部电影显示了一个裂谷的存在。
“一张照片胜过千言万语，眼见为实，这句老话是真的，”萨普在1999年的一篇回顾文章中评论道。
是什么造成了裂痕呢？赫斯在1962年的一篇论文中提出了一些想法。它假设热的岩浆从裂谷处的地球内部升起，随着冷却而膨胀，并将相邻的两个板块推向更远的地方。这一观点是对板块构造理论的关键贡献，但赫斯没有参考“海底”中提出的批判性工作--这是为数不多的将萨普列为合著者的出版物之一。
萨普继续与希岑合作，使海底栩栩如生。他们的合作包括1967年由国家地理出版的印度洋地图，以及1977年世界海洋地底地图，现在保存在国会图书馆。
1977年希岑去世后，萨普继续她的工作，直到2006年去世。1978年10月，Heezen(死后)和Tharp被授予哈伯德奖章，这是国家地理学会的最高荣誉，加入了欧内斯特·沙克尔顿、路易斯和玛丽·利基以及简·古德尔等探险家和发现者的行列。
今天，船只使用一种叫做条带映射的方法，这种方法测量带状路径上的深度，而不是沿着一条线。这些丝带可以缝合在一起，以创建一张准确的海底地图。
但由于船只移动缓慢，一艘船需要200年才能完全绘制出海底地图。一项在2030年之前绘制整个海底详细地图的国际努力正在进行中，该努力使用了多艘船，由日本基金会(Nippon Foundation)和海洋总等深图(General Bathmetic Chart Of The Ocean)牵头。
这些信息对于开始了解邻里尺度上的海底是什么样子至关重要。萨普是第一个展示海底及其不同社区丰富地形的人。
苏珊娜·奥康奈尔(Suzanne OConnell)是卫斯理大学(Wesleyan University)地球与环境科学教授。本文最初发表在theconversation.com上。
note_en:
Despite all the deep-sea expeditions and samples taken from the seabed in the past 100 years, humans still know little about the ocean’s deepest reaches. And there are good reasons to learn more.
 Most  tsunamis start with earthquakes under or near the ocean floor. The seafloor provides habitat for fish, corals and  complex communities of microbes, crustaceans and other organisms. Its topography controls currents that  distribute heat, helping to regulate Earth’s climate.
 July 30 marked the 100th anniversary of the birth of Marie Tharp, a geologist and oceanographer who created maps that changed the way people imagine two-thirds of the world. Beginning in 1957, Tharp and her research partner, geologist Bruce Heezen, began publishing the first comprehensive maps that showed the main features of the ocean bottom — mountains, valleys and trenches.
   As a  geoscientist, I believe Tharp should be as famous as Jane Goodall or Neil Armstrong. Here’s why.
  Well into the 1950s, many scientists assumed the seabed was featureless. Tharp showed that it contained rugged terrain, and that much of it was laid out in a systematic way.
 Her images were critical to the development of plate tectonic theory — the idea that plates, or large sections of Earth’s crust, interact to generate the planet’s seismic and volcanic activity. Earlier researchers — particularly geophysicist and meteorologist Alfred Wegener — noticed how well the coastlines of Africa and South America fit together and proposed the continents had once been connected. Tharp identified mountains and a rift valley in the center of the Atlantic Ocean where the two continents could have been ripped apart.
   Thanks to Tharp’s hand-drawn renditions of the ocean floor, I can imagine a walk across the Atlantic Ocean bottom from New York to Lisbon. The journey would take me out along the continental shelf. Then downward toward the  Sohm Abyssal Plain. I’d need to detour around underwater mountains, called seamounts. Then I’d start a slow climb up the  Mid-Atlantic Ridge, a submerged north-south mountain range.
 After ascending to 8,200 feet below sea level to the ridge’s peak, I would descend several hundred feet, cross the ridge’s central rift valley and proceed up over the ridge’s eastern edge. Then back down to the ocean floor, until I began trekking up the European continental slope to Lisbon. The total walk would be about  3,800 miles — almost twice the length of the Appalachian Trail.
  Born in Ypsilanti, Mich., Tharp studied English and music in college. But then in 1943 she enrolled in a University of Michigan master’s degree program designed to train women to be petroleum geologists during World War II.
   “Girls were needed to fill the jobs left open because the guys were off fighting,”  Tharp later recalled.
 After working for an oil company in Oklahoma, Tharp sought a geology job at Columbia University in 1948. Women couldn’t go on research ships, but Tharp could draft, and was hired to assist male graduate students.
 Tharp worked with Heezen, a graduate student who gave her seafloor profiles to draft. These are long paper rolls that show the depth of the seafloor along a linear path, measured from a ship using sonar.
 Starting with a large blank sheet of paper, Tharp marked lines of latitude and longitude. Then she’d carefully mark where the ship had traveled. Next she’d read the depth at each location off the sonar profile, mark it on the ship’s track and create her own condensed profile, showing the depth to the ocean floor vs. the distance the ship had traveled.
   One of her important innovations was creating sketches depicting what the seafloor would look like. These views made it easier to visualize the ocean floor’s topography and create a physiographic map.
 Tharp’s careful plotting of six east-to-west profiles across the North Atlantic revealed something no one had described before: a cleft in the center of the ocean, miles wide and hundreds of feet deep. Tharp suggested that it was a rift valley — a type of long trough that was known to exist on land.
 Heezen  called this idea “girl talk” and told Tharp to recalculate and redraft. When she did, the rift valley was still there.
 Another research assistant was plotting locations of earthquake epicenters on a map of the same size and scale. Comparing the two maps, Heezen and Tharp realized that the earthquake epicenters fell inside the rift valley. This discovery was critical to the development of plate tectonic theory: It suggested that movement was occurring in the rift valley, and that the continents might actually be drifting apart.
   This insight was revolutionary. When Heezen, as a newly-minted PhD, gave a talk at Princeton in 1957 and showed the rift valley and epicenters, geology department chair  Harry Hess replied, “You have shaken the foundations of geology.”
  In 1959, the Geological Society of America published “ The Floors of the Oceans: I. The North Atlantic” by Heezen, Tharp and Maurice “Doc” Ewing, director of the Lamont Geological Observatory, where they worked. It contained Tharp’s ocean profiles, ideas and access to Tharp’s physiographic maps.
 Some scientists thought the work was brilliant, but most didn’t believe it. French undersea explorer Jacques Cousteau was determined to prove Tharp wrong. Sailing aboard his research vessel, the  Calypso, he purposely crossed the Mid-Atlantic Ridge and lowered an underwater movie camera. To Cousteau’s surprise, the film showed that a rift valley existed.
   “There’s truth to the old cliche that a picture is worth a thousand words and that seeing is believing,” Tharp observed in a  1999 retrospective essay.
 What could have created the rift? Hess proposed some ideas in a  1962 paper. It postulated that hot magma rose from inside the Earth at the rift, expanded as it cooled and pushed two adjoining plates farther apart. This idea was a key contribution to plate tectonic theory, but Hess failed to reference the critical work presented in “The Floors of the Oceans” — one of the few publications that included Tharp as a co-author.
  Tharp continued working with Heezen to bring the ocean floor to life. Their collaboration included an  Indian Ocean map, published by National Geographic in 1967, and a 1977  World Ocean Floor map that is now held at the Library of Congress.
   After Heezen died in 1977, Tharp continued her work until her death in 2006. In October 1978, Heezen (posthumously) and Tharp were awarded the  Hubbard Medal, the National Geographic Society’s highest honor, joining the ranks of explorers and discoverers such as Ernest Shackleton, Louis and Mary Leakey, and Jane Goodall.
 Today, ships use a  method called swath mapping, which measures depth over a ribbonlike path rather than along a single line. The ribbons can be stitched together to create an accurate seafloor map.
 But because ships move slowly, it would take one ship 200 years to completely map the seafloor. An international effort to map the entire ocean floor in detail by 2030 is underway, using multiple ships, led by the  Nippon Foundation and the  General Bathymetric Chart of the Oceans.
 This information is critical to beginning to understand what the seafloor looks like on a neighborhood scale. Tharp was the first person to show the rich topography of the ocean floor and its different neighborhoods.
  Suzanne OConnell is professor of earth and environmental sciences at Wesleyan University. This article was originally published on  theconversation.com.
title: 玛丽·萨普的地图彻底改变了我们对海底的认识
title_en: Marie Tharp’s maps revolutionized our knowledge of the seafloor
